coredata.dc:title: Recent trends and insights into carbon dots dispersed liquid crystal composites
coredata.dc:creator: Neha,
Singh, G.
Kumar, S.
Malik, Praveen
Supreet,
coredata.dc:description: The technique of dispersing nanomaterials into liquid crystal (LC) matrix to improve their various physical parameters appears to be undergoing a paradigm change towards a more cost-effective and environmentally friendly way to create superior electro-optic devices. Over the past decade, due to their extraordinary properties, Carbon Dots (CDs) have created a bustle in the area of research in nanoscience, nanotechnology, and allied fields. Dispersion of CDs in LC matrix to enhance their various physical properties has emerged as a hot topic of research because the results achieved so far are highly promising for device applications. Therefore, it is worth studying and analyzing the impact of dispersing CDs in the LC media on various physical features of the host and vice versa. In this article, we have provided a comprehensive review of the current achievements and insights in CDs dispersed LC composites. The review starts with the discussion on the unique outstanding properties of CDs and their synthesis methods. Further, a systematic review of the effects of CDs on tuning the optical, dielectric, and electro-optical properties of nematic and ferroelectric LCs has been carried out. In the last section, current issues faced in this area and future prospects of CDs-LC composites have also been discussed. Overall, detail studies shows that CDs are a better, cost-effective, and eco-friendly alternative dopant in the LC matrix as compared to conventional nanomaterials, including heavy metal-based Quantum Dots. Hence, CDs-LC composites have the potential to provide a better solution for low-cost, high-performance, and environmentally safe LC-based high-performance electro-optical and photonic applications.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The technique of dispersing nanomaterials into liquid crystal (LC) matrix to improve their various physical parameters appears to be undergoing a paradigm change towards a more cost-effective and environmentally friendly way to create superior electro-optic devices. Over the past decade, due to their extraordinary properties, Carbon Dots (CDs) have created a bustle in the area of research in nanoscience, nanotechnology, and allied fields. Dispersion of CDs in LC matrix to enhance their various physical properties has emerged as a hot topic of research because the results achieved so far are highly promising for device applications. Therefore, it is worth studying and analyzing the impact of dispersing CDs in the LC media on various physical features of the host and vice versa. In this article, we have provided a comprehensive review of the current achievements and insights in CDs dispersed LC composites. The review starts with the discussion on the unique outstanding properties of CDs and their synthesis methods. Further, a systematic review of the effects of CDs on tuning the optical, dielectric, and electro-optical properties of nematic and ferroelectric LCs has been carried out. In the last section, current issues faced in this area and future prospects of CDs-LC composites have also been discussed. Overall, detail studies shows that CDs are a better, cost-effective, and eco-friendly alternative dopant in the LC matrix as compared to conventional nanomaterials, including heavy metal-based Quantum Dots. Hence, CDs-LC composites have the potential to provide a better solution for low-cost, high-performance, and environmentally safe LC-based high-performance electro-optical and photonic applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Liquid Crystals (LCs) are intriguing organic materials that exist in a state that is halfway between an ordered crystalline solid and an isotropic liquid [1]. Both liquid and crystalline characteristics are simultaneously present in LCs. Liquid crystals (LCs) have some characteristics reminiscent of disordered liquids, which include significant fluidity, creation, and agglomeration of droplets; yet, LCs, like crystals, display anisotropy in their optic, physical, electric, and magnetic properties. Due to their anisotropy in shape, LC molecules have a propensity to align in just one favoured direction, termed as the “director” (n). The physical characteristics of LCs can also be easily tuned by tunning the LC director orientation with the application of external electric, magnetic fields and also with heat. These characteristics are crucial for the operation of LCs-based products such as smart watches, calculators, flat TV screens, thermosensors, LC displays (LCDs), etc.

LCs are broadly classified as thermotropic or lyotropic LCs based on variations in thermodynamical parameters such as temperature, concentration etc., which are responsible for liquid crystalline phase transition. Phase transitions which are influenced by temperature and pressure result in the formation of thermotropic LCs, and when these transitions are dependent on the concentration of solvent as well, they result in lyotropic LCs. Further, LCs are classified on the basis of molecular shape and structure, they can be further categorized as rod-shaped (calamitic), disc-shaped (discotic), bent-core, brick, or lath-shaped (sanidic) (Fig. 1 ) [2]. Many of the thermotropic LCs display a range of mesophases with the change in the temperature. For example, as the temperature is increased, certain mesogens could display smectic, nematic, and finally isotropic phases [3]. During these transitions, multiple mesophases are observed based on the chemical composition, order, and symmetry of constituents.

The nematic phase is the most frequently observed in thermotropic liquid crystals. The molecules in the nematic phase possess long-range orientational order but no positional order. As a result, the molecules flowin such a way that the positions of their centers of mass are randomly oriented, just like in a liquid, yet the longer axes of the molecules tend to align in the same direction (director, n), just like they do in a crystal. The term “nematics” comes from the Greek word “nema,” which translates to “thread” due to their thread-like appearance under a polarising optical microscope. When subjected to external (electrical/magnetic/optical) fields, the molecular orientation in NLC can be altered, resulting in the tuning of their electrical, optical, and electro-optical characteristics very easily and conveniently. Cholesteric mesophase is a special class of nematic phase which is observed when a very minute quantity of chiral molecules incorporated into the nematic phase, resulting into a twist in its structure in a helical manner. This helical structure causes director n to define a helical trajectory along an axis with a helical pitch (p) of the order of a few microns. It has been observed in some LCs that with a decrease in the temperature, smectic phases appear to have one-dimensional positional order along with the orientational order of the nematic phase. In the sematic phase, constituent molecules are present in the form of well-arranged layers, and these layers have a tendency to glide relative to one over the other, which is responsible for their fluidic nature. The smectic term originated from the Greek word 'smegma', which indicates a cleaning or soap-like nature. They have a well-defined spacing between their layers, and the longer molecular axis either aligns up normal (smectic A, SmA) or inclines at some angle (smectic C, SmC) to the layer planes. Depending upon their structure details, various smectic phases exist. In contrast to the biaxial character of the SmC phase, the SmA phase is uniaxial. The chiral smectic C (SmC*) phase is an SmC phase composed of chiral molecules. The chiral molecules turn the projection direction of the director in each layer, resulting in the creation of a helical structure. As a result, these layers rotate from one to the other since the orientation of their molecules is neither parallel nor perpendicular to the layer of SmC*. Hence, these phases are optically positive uniaxial, displaying optical activity and selective reflection similar to the cholesteric LC phase. The molecules in the SmC* phase exhibit a persistent dipole moment normal to their long axes, resulting in ferroelectric properties, which are commonly referred to as ferroelectric liquid crystals (FLCs) [4].

Interesting findings of research on the electro-optical and electrical features of these unique LC compounds have sparked the curiosity of the scientific community and industrial sectors. Since then LCs have been exploited in the field of display and other electro-optical devices. Despite the great potential of LCs in various technological, scientific, and medical fields, there is a constant need to improve upon their properties to meet the market demand. For any LC-based electro-optical (or display) device application, desirable features are good contrast ratio, higher brightness, low threshold voltage, low power consumption, high viewing angle, good luminescence, defect-free alignment, ionic impurities-free, and low response time. To enhance these properties and to improve device performance, either a new LC with the desired properties should be synthesized or the simple process of dispersing some suitable nanomaterials, polymers, dyes, etc. into LC hosts, which tune their properties could be utilized.

The revolutionary breakthroughs in nanoscience and technology have paved the way for the emergence of the hybridization of LCs and nanomaterials, resulting in LC-nanocomposites, which have drawn the interest of researchers during the last few decades. Nanomaterials’ unique size-dependent optical, electrical, chemical, and magnetic properties make them suitable materials for applications in practically every aspect of science and technology, from optics, computing, and energy to biosciences, catalysis, and medical science [5,6]. Till date, various types of nanomaterials have been employed as dopants in LCs: semiconductor [7–12], ferroelectric [13,14], metals [15–18], and carbon [19–22]. Several studies showed that dispersing such types of nanomaterials in the LC host improves their electro-optical response, alignment, optical and dielectric parameters significantly [23–28]. The field of LC nanocomposites has seen a paradigm shift towards the incorporation of quantum dots (QDs) as dopants in LC host media. But there is always a continuous strive to find sustainable alternates to existing technology. Currently, carbon dots (CDs) have arisen as astate-of-the-art carbon-based nanomaterial due to their assortedphysio-chemical properties and distinctive characteristics such as excellent biocompatibility, distinct optical characteristics, low price, environmentally-safe, multiple functionalgroups (such as amino, carbonyl, epoxy), andhigh stability. Nowadays, researchers around the world are focusing on harnessing these properties of CDs along with those of LC to prepare CDs-LC composites for various electro-optical and photonic device applications with improved performance.

In this review, aiming at a distinct outlook, we have focused on the recent developments in the area of CDs-LC composites. First, we have discussed the outstanding optical characteristics of CDs and the current advancements in their synthesis methods. The potential applications of CDs have also been discussed and then followed by a brief description of the methods of preparation of CDs-LC composites. The next section of this review concentrates on the recent advancements in the area of CDs-LC composites. This section is divided into two parts depending upon the type of LC used as the host medium i.e. (i) CDs dispersed nematic liquid crystal and (ii) CDs dispersed ferroelectric liquid crystal. Overall, it has been found that dispersing CDs in LC media has significantly altered the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of LC, along with improvement in the alignment of LCs, switching response, dielectric properties, etc. Finally, we have discussed our perspective on addressing some of the challenges associated with CDs-LC composites and their plausible solutions. We hope that this review would certainly shed light on how CDs help in tuning the properties of LCs and how to utilize these composites for the production of LC based display and other electro-optical device which would be more sustainable, eco-friendly, affordable, tunable, and versatile.

CDs, a novel functional material of the carbon-based nanomaterial family, generally have a spherical shape and dimensions smaller than 10 nm, and possess magnificent physical and chemical properties. CDs have gained huge attention in a very short span of time as compared to other different types of carbon nanomaterials, owing to their extraordinary features. CDs’ synthesis techniques are relatively easy and involve low cost, surface functionalization is also easy and CDs possess tunable luminescence property. In addition to these benefits, CDs are environmentally sustainable as they possess low toxicity, high water-solubility, biocompatibility and stability at room temperature. These characteristic of CDs give them an upper hand over other well-known quantum dots (QDs) such as metal oxide QDs (SnO2, TiO2) and inorganic QDs (CuInS2/ZnS, InP/ZnS). CDs were first reported by Xu et al. [29], they were discovered accidentally during the process of purification of single-wall carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs). After 2 years, the first stable photoluminescent carbon nanoparticles of different dimensions were reported by Sun et al. dubbed them as “carbon quantum dots (CQDs)” [30]. Generally, CDs have a spherical shape and consist of both sp2 and sp3 hybridized structures. Furthermore, their functionalization can be easily done to tune their properties, with carboxyl, carbonyl, hydroxyl, amino, and epoxy groups over the surfaces, which also provides them good binding capability with organic as well as inorganic moieties [31].

Generally, CDs are divided into three categories: carbon nano-dots (CNDs), graphene quantum dots (GQDs), and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), depending on their structure, surface functionalization groups, and physical characteristics. In the case of CNDs, the variety and intricacy of the core structure ranges from amorphous to crystalline. Crystalline CDs are usually quasi-spherical nanostructures and are superior to amorphous ones based on their structure and catalytic properties and are known as CQDs. GQDs are made up of a few atomically thin graphene sheets functionalized with chemical moieties on the edges. However, CPDs are basically cross-linked monohybrids of carbon and aggregated polymers with a central carbonized core surrounded by either the polymeric chains or functional groups. Despite their differences in structure, size, and surface functional groups, it is found that all varieties of CDs display equivalent PL properties. In this review, we shall be focusing on CNDs and CQDs only.

ce:list-item.ce:label: I.
ce:list-item.ce:para: Optical absorption: The optical absorption spectra of CDs lies in the ultra-violet region (in the range of 260–320 nm) owing to the п–п* transition of C = C bonds. An extended tail has also been observed in the visible region. CDs produced from MW-CNTs through electrochemical oxidation reported to have an absorbance band of 270 nm with an FWHM of 50 nm [32]. According to recent research, the absorption spectra exhibit a red shift following a particular surface treatment [33].

ce:list-item.ce:label: II.
ce:list-item.ce:para: Photoluminescence (PL): Photoluminescence is another most fascinating feature of CDs which utilize its application in the field of photocatalysis. Like Stokes emission, the PL emission has a longer emission wavelength than its excitation wavelength. It has been observed that the size of CDs also governs PL emission [34]. Li and group, reported intense brilliant blue, green, yellow, and red PL of CQDs by varying the size of CQDs [35]. As shown in Fig. 2 , a redshift in the emission wavelength has been noticed in the PL analysis of CDs when the size of the CDs is reduced.

ce:section.ce:para:
CDs also possess photoinduced redox properties which were demonstrated by Wang et al. Results showed that photo-excited CDs exhibit excellent properties of both electrons donation and electrons acceptance, thus creating wide opportunities for their applications in light-energy conversion and other related fields [36].
III.
Chemiluminescence (CL) and Electrochemiluminescence (ECL): CL is one of the types of luminescence in which the emission of light takes place during redox chemical reactions between the reagents. CDs act as oxidants in the CL reactions and the emitted entities get engaged in redox oxidation in the process. During this whole process, the electron-hole annihilation process is accelerated and energy is released as CL emission. In one of the reports, CL properties of fluorescent CDs have been demonstrated when acidic potassium permanganate and cerium (IV) act as oxidants. CL emission has been associated with the radiative recombination of oxidant-injected holes and electrons in CDs [37]. It was also observed that by altering the surface groups of CDs, the CL characteristics of the CDs could be modified. Also, CL properties of CDs make CDs as one of the new promising materials for Electrochemiluminescence (ECL) hence opening up the budding field of development of new-generation ECL emitting species. ECL is a process of transforming electrical energy into radiative energy. In this process, a luminescent signal is produced when applied high voltage created species at electrodes execute high-energy electron-transfer reactions [38].
IV.
Up-converted photoluminescence (UCPL): Along with typical PL emission, certain CDs exhibit up-converted photoluminescence (UCPL). The UCPL refers to the process in which low-energy photons are transformed into high-energy photons, i.e., the shorter wavelength is emitted in comparison to the excitation wavelength via consecutive interactions inside a medium [39]. Cao and colleagues reported the UCPL property of CQDs synthesized through laser ablation and observed a strong luminescence along with two-photon excitation near-infrared region (800 nm) [40]. Furthermore, Kang et al. also observed the UPCL, as well as normal PL emission in the case of CQDs prepared from glucose via ultrasonic-assisted treatment [41]. Because of the UPCL property of CQDs, they have fascinating applications in various fields, like bioscience and energy technology, as they can be used for cell imaging via two-photon luminescence microscopy and also for more efficient catalyst design.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Methods for the synthesis of CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
A wide variety of synthesis techniques has been reported for low-cost production of high-quality CDs. Based on the starting carbon source and the final product formed, CD synthesis approaches are generally, divided into two categories termed as the top-down and bottom-up approaches. Top-down procedures involve the physical or chemical transformation of chunks of carbonaceous materials like graphite, carbon nanotubes, and others into CDs. Laser ablation, chemical ablation, electrochemical therapy, and ultrasonic aided procedures are all part of the top-down strategy. In bottom-up approach, CDs are produced at molecular level using sucrose, glucose, citric acid, etc. using microwave synthesis, hydrothermal treatment, and thermal deposition techniques. Some of the most frequently used method to synthesis CDs have been summarised as follow:
I.
Hydrothermal: The hydrothermal method is a bottom-up strategy for the synthesis of a diverse range of carbon-based nanomaterials from various starting materials. Animal products (cow milk and egg albumin), acids (citric, ascorbic, and gelatin), grass, food caramels, orange juice, coffee seeds, bananas, honey, soy milk, chitosan, honey, watermelon peels, cellulose, starch, pomelo peel, starch, and paper ash have all been reported to be used in the one-step fabrication of self-passivated fluorescent CDs [42]. It is a less expensive, environmentally friendly and non-toxic technique. Zhang et al. reported fluorescent CDs (size ∼ 2 nm) production via hydrothermal treatment from L-ascorbic acid in 2010 [43]. Bhunia and co-workers described the synthesis of hydrophobic and hydrophilic CQDs via carbohydrate carbonization [44].
II.
Laser Ablation: Laser ablation is a sort of synthesis process that includes utilizing a laser source to irradiate a carbon target. Sun et al. created CDs using laser ablation of graphite when water vapours were present with argon flow [30]. According to the findings of Hu et al., the size of CQDs can be altered through the manipulation of the laser pulse width [45]. One of the advantages of this technology is the adjustable size morphology of the generated CQDs. The size of the carbon nanoparticles produced by laser irradiation varied widely, larger sizes were easily rejected during centrifugation, resulting in lower yields of carbon nanoparticles and low carbon material utilization efficiency.
III.
Electrochemical Carbonization: This is the most widely used method for synthesising CDs due to its high purity, high yield, strong repeatability, good size control, and low cost. First, Zhou et al. described the electrochemical production of CQDs from multi-walled carbon nanotubes [32].
IV.
Thermal decomposition: It has recently been discovered that CDs can be made by dehydrating and carbonising organic molecules using external heat. This method features a solvent-free approach, is simple to operate, has a short reaction time, is low in cost, has a wide precursor tolerance, and is scalable in production [46]. Martindale et al. produced CQDs by pyrolysis of citric acid at 180 °C for 40 h with a 45 % yield [47].
V.
Microwave synthesis: Zhu et al. reported the microwave pyrolysis synthesis of CDs, which is a quick, inexpensive, and environmentally safe method [48]. The microwave assisted CDs synthesis technique has many advantages over other techniques like less time and energy consumption, no sophisticated equipment requirement, and is a well-regulated process.
VI.
Chemical oxidation: Chemical oxidation, which involves oxidizinga substrate using an oxidative reagent like H2SO4 or HNO3, is another simple and efficient method for large scale synthesis of CDs. Qiao et al. [49] and Tian’s group [50] used commercially activated carbon as carbon sources and natural gas soot respectively to synthesis CDs in the presence of nitric acid.

As already discussed, CDs possess many unique optical and electrical properties, and due to their ease of preparation, various groups around the world are working on various techniques to further improve upon these properties. The main basic elements of CDs are carbon, hydrogen, and oxygen. The proportion of these elements in CDs are determined by the synthesis route followed. Doping of hetero-atoms in CDs has opened up a new era to improvise the chemical composition, physical and structural properties of CDs, etc. It was already observed that doping CDs with heteroatoms (N, S, P, B, etc.) enhance their quantum yield and allows for long-wavelength emission. Doping highly electronegative atoms (N and S) in CDs causes a blue shift in PL emission, whereas doping with less electronegative atoms (P and B) causes a redshift [51]. According to the studies, N doping can increase the surface defect of CDs, hence tuning their optical characteristics and chemical reactivity [52]. P doping results in enhancing the electronic properties of CDs and also boosts fluorescence quality and stability since it generates an abundance of activation sites [53]. These characteristics make them a good option for photovoltaic devices. In contrast to N and P, which function as n-type dopants and result in increased concentration of electrons in CDs, B doping causes a decrease in the energy state of CDs due to the electron deficit nature of B. Researchers reported the modification in the optical characteristics of CD with B doping because it provides a large number of active sites, which led to its application in the fields of display and lighting [54].

CDs have excellent and stable optical properties along with controlled and tunable fluorescent features, biocompatibility, resistant to photo-bleaching, non-toxicity, and good UV absorption ability [55]. The presence of numerous carboxyl moieties on the surface of CD is responsible for their good stability and biocompatibility. CDs are emerging as a new hotspot area in the domains of bio-imaging, drug delivery, biosensing, photocatalysis, photovoltaic devices, photodynamic therapy, and optoelectronics. CL and ECL are caused by the exceptional electronic features of CDs and can be employed in light-energy conversion and related fields [56]. They are the faces of a new generation of PL and photo-detecting materials. CDs can be used in dye-sensitized solar cells (DSSCs) and organic solar cells (OSCs) owingto their exceptional electro-optical characteristics. For the first time, Ozin and group introduced CDs as a sensitizer in DSSCs that collect sunlight [57]. CDs-based hybrid networks have recently been identified to be excellent electrode materials for supercapacitors [58]. CDs are widely used as LED materials becauseof their low cost, reliable light emission qualities, and low environmental impact [59]. Other remarkable applications of CDs include photovoltaic conversion [60], energy storage [61], and photochemical transformation [62]. Wang et al. have recently used the Sulphur doped CDs fluorescence sensor to detect quantitatively Hg2+ in tap water with high sensitivity and selectivity [63]. Numerous investigations have revealed that heteroatom-doped CDs possess broad wavelengths and multicoloredfluorescence, and these characteristics make them suitable for use in applications including persistent and real-time cell scanning as well as in vivo biological imaging. Gao et al. reported that Biocompatible N-CDs can be used to identify PC12 cells [64].

Apart from these applications, in the last few years, CDs have also been used as dopants in LC matrix to prepare CDs-LC composites with improved physical properties which could be further utilized for various device applications. In this article, our aim is to focus on the review of recent work done in the area of CDs-NLCs and CDs-FLC composites.

To prepare CDs-LC composites, known weight of CDs are first dispersed in known volume of a solvent (typically toluene) and then sonicated to reduce the chances of aggregation of CDs. Further required concentration of CDs (optimized wt. / wt. %) is added to the selected LC host. The resulting mixture is ultra-sonicated for roughly 2 h at a temperature greater than the isotropic temperature of LC to assure the homogeneous dispersion of CDs in the LC matrix. Followed by evaporation of the solvent, the composite is further heated a little above isotropic temperature on a hot plate and cooled down to make uniform mixing. Then such a uniform composite of CDs and LC molecules is filled via capillary action in a sample cell, fabricated from conducting ITO coated glass plates. Afterward, various characterizations such as optical, electro-optical, and dielectric can be carried out to study these composites.

The possibility to tune the properties by simply mixing liquid crystalline and carbon-based nanomaterials seems very interesting and has opened new horizons in the field of LC research. Prior to the discovery of CDs, attention was focused on tuning the properties of LCs by carbon-based nanomaterials such CNTs, fullerene, graphene, and graphene oxide. Many research publications have demonstrated that carbon nanotubes (CNTs) doping in LC matrix has a favourable impact on various LC parameters likefaster response times, improved dielectric properties, decreased amplitude of residual DC, and low threshold voltage, among others [65–68]. Because of the ion trapping property of CNTs, the concentration of free ions in the solvent is reduced, resulting in raising the applied cell voltage and reducing the electric field shielding. This ion trapping feature, as well as the high permittivity and anisotropy of CNTs, contributed to improvement in electro-optical performance of LC composites. According to a research, fullerene is a strong electron acceptor with the ability to accept one to six electrons and resulting in the creation of corresponding anions [69]. Various researchers have highlighted the pivotal role played by fullerene in enhancing the electrical and electro-optical properties of LC composites [70,71]. Several studies have also shown that LC devices perform better when doped with graphene oxide (GO) [72,73]. The interaction of an electrical dipole on the surface of GO with an electrical dipole on the surface of LC, as well as the capacity of GO to trap free ions, could be the cause of this better performance. Furthermore, reports showed that the physical properties of LC can also be modified by doping them with other carbon-based nanomaterials such as detonation nano-diamond [74] and graphene quantum dots [75].
In the present review paper, we will primarily concentrate on the dispersion of CDs in Nematic and Ferroelectric LCs and its effect on tuning the properties of LCs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon dots dispersed nematic liquid crystals composites
ce:section.ce:para: The most difficult issue indealing with QDs-LC composites in various fields is the synthesis and utilization of minimally hazardous QDs. As previously discussed, CDs could be a good replacement for hazardous QDs already reported in QDs-LC composite systems. Khan et al. [76] doped bent-core nematic liquid crystal (BCNLC, 6–2 M−F) with CDs (∼5–6 nm) and studied their dielectric and elastic properties. Generally, in the case of BCNLC, the bent elastic constant (K33) value is less than the splay elastic constant (K 11) value, such that elastic anisotropy (ΔK = K33 – K 11) is negative. In bent core NLC, K33 is less than K 11 because the LC molecule has some curvature at its core due to the existence of short-range smectic-C (SmC) type correlations in the N phase. But interestingly, authors reported the inversion of sign for ΔK by doping BCNLC with 0.1 % of CDs (size ∼ 5–6 nm) as shown in Fig. 3 . Authors proposed that CDs generated an anisotropic chain-like pattern by self-assembly when the temperature was dropped below 130 °C, resulting in the reduction of splay and bending deformations, and raising the value of the elastic anisotropy.

Other significant findings of this study showed a lower threshold voltage for the composites as a result of interaction between dipole moment of CDs and BCNLC causing the induction of a stronger local electric field which reoriented the LC molecules.

When LCs are doped with semiconductor QDs, magnetic, and ferroelectric nanoparticles, these composites exhibit unique non-linear optical features such as non-linear refraction (NLR), non-linear absorption (NLA), and photorefractive efficiency [77–79]. Nonlinearity in optical properties is also observed in LCs when doped with CDs, making them a good choice for non-linear photonic devices. In recent research, Praseetha et al. [80] reported the third-order non-linear optical properties of the CDs-NLC composite for the first time. The authors prepared four composites by doping varying concentrations of organophilic CDs (∼8–10 nm) in NLC (5PCH, clearing temperature TC = 57° C) containing 0.1, 0.2, 0.4, and 1 wt% CDs. Non-linear optical investigations showed that when the concentration of CDs in the composite increases, the NLA is enhanced because the internal interaction between the excited CDs and the NLC causes aberrant optical reorientation of molecules in NLC composites. It had a maximum value for samples containing 0.2 wt% CDs and then decreased with increasing dopant concentration as NPs agglomeration occurred, causing hindrances in NLC reorientation when the optical field was present (Fig. 4 ). In Table 1 , the measured values of βeff (non-linear absorption coefficient) and χ3 (third order susceptibility) obtained via open aperture (OA) curve fitting for pure and CDs-NLC composites are presented.

Also, the observed closed aperture (CA) Z-scan of doped LC showed a pre-focal transmission peak along with a post-focal transmission valley, indicating that the sample had a negative refractive non-linear nature. The CA Z-scan graphs illustrated the self-defocusing property of composites, which increases until the concentration of CDs approached 0.2 wt%, after which it began to decrease. The remarkable enhanced value of NLR could be explained by both (a) the charge transfer mechanism (which occurs between LC molecules and dopant CDs) and (b) the Janossy effect. Authors suggested that these CDs-5PCH LC composites have the potential to act as an optical limiter (Fig. 5 ). The composite with 0.2 wt% dopant has a lower threshold value for optical limiter functioning when compared to pure 5PCH LC, which makes them suitable candidates for applications in optical sensors and other photonic applications.

As CDs are chemically and thermally stable, they may be a superior substitute to semiconductor QDs in enhancing the electro-optical properties of LCs as already discussed. Taking this into consideration, Urbanski et al. demonstrated the electro-optical characteristics of a CDs doped LC system [81]. Four samples of CDs (2.5 nm) in LC1 (Felix-2900–03) with different dopant concentrations of 0.5, 1.0, 2.5, and 5.0 wt% were prepared and reported the spatial distribution of CDs in LC1. POM studies revealed that on dispersing CDs into LC1, the phase transition temperature was found to be reduced and the nematic-isotropic biphasic temperature region was found to be broadened for all composites (Fig. 6 ). An electro-optical study indicated a drop in threshold voltage as CDs produced pre-tilt, causing director deformation of LC molecules and hence reduction in dielectric anisotropy. At high dopant concentrations (5 wt%) and low temperatures, an initial tilt angle of up to 8° was observed. However, as the temperature was raised, the effect on boundary conditions got reduced. But it was observed that no considerable rise in the tilt angle was noticed with low dopant concentration.

Most recently, in an ongoing effort to understand the mechanisms underlying interactions involving CDs and LC, Pandey and co-workers investigated the optical properties and zeta-potential of CQDs by dispersing them in NLC (7CB) [82]. The CQDs-NLC composites were also studied using time-resolved and steady-state fluorescence and UV spectroscopy. The samples with concentrations of 0.1, 0.2, and 0.5 wt% CQDs (∼1–3 nm) doped in NLC (7CB) were used in their investigations. For pure 7CB, the authors reported that the zeta potential value was the lowest, while it was the highest for CQDs dispersed composite with a 0.2 wt% concentration. A higher zeta potential in the composite systems made them more stable than the pure 7CB because of the stronger repulsive forces between their molecules. The electrostatic repulsive forces along with LC-CQDs and CQD-CQD interactions had a significant role in 0.2 wt% CQDs-LC composite because the net surface charge on both CQD and LC was negative. CQDs aggregation at greater concentrations (0.5 wt%) resulted in clusters that reduced momentum, lowering the charge transfer mechanism between CQDs and LC, and however, dipolar interaction between the two was prominent. They claimed that higher zeta potential for intermediate concentrations of CQDs-NLC composites implied the possibility of other electro-kinetic interactions such as DEP (dielectrophoretic forces) and EP (electrophoretic forces) that generated the complex structure of composites.

The study also found that the electrophoretic mobility and conductivity were higher for composite with 0.2 wt% CQDs. These parameters increase as the concentration of CQDs in the host grew, which may be related to LC-CQDs, CQDs-CQDs, and other electrostatic and dispersion interactions. In PL studies, authors noticed the quenching of PL peak in 7CB LC composites and found it to be more notable in the composite containing 0.5 wt% CQDs concentration. Results showed that the quenching is significant while the shape of the peak is unaltered (Fig. 9 ). According to the authors, cross-linked molecular clusters of CQDs were formed in composites, and charge transfer from close-surface LC molecules to CQDs was less efficient because of the presence of electron-accepting and electron-donating surface-functionalized groups leading to the luminescence quenching. The authors concluded that CQDs play the role of quencher for NLC (7CB) and the behaviour of quenching was analysed by plotting I0/I versus CQDs concentration (Fig. 10 ), which was found to follow the Stern-Volmer equation for quenching (Q):

Where, I0 and I represent the fluorescence intensity in the absence and presence of the NPs, respectively, Here α(Q) represents the fitting parameter and Ksv(Q) is Stern-Volmer quenching constant.

For low concentrations (0.1 and 0.2 wt%) CQDs in 7CB quenching responded in a static manner whereas at high concentration quenching exhibited non-linear nature. The inter-particle interaction between NPs becomes significant at larger concentrations and hinders the linear quenching process. The aggregation of dopants, the development of heterogeneous structures, the formation of domains, defects, etc., are some physical factors that have an impact on the linear behaviour of optical properties with the variation of doping concentration and cause non-linearity in them. Frank and Vavilov incorporated the idea of a “sphere-of-action” into the Stern-Volmer equation when the concentration of quencher grows, which leads to nonlinearity. There are always quencher molecules within a charge transfer distance in resultant complexdue to the increasedconcentration of quenchers, which have a less mean separation; in other words, quenchers always exist inside the liquid crystal's field of influence [83].
However, more detailed studies are required to explain this quenching phenomenon which will help to gain relevant facts about the structure and behaviour of biological macromolecular systems.

Recently, CQDs based on Oil Palm Leaf (OPL CQDs) have been reported as a novel class of nano-scale materials used in the electronic field. In addition to the advantageous properties possessed by CDs, OPL CQDs have many charge trap sites which make them more efficient charge carrier pockets, making them a possible contender for use in electrical storage devices, supercapacitors, energy conversion, and other applications [84,85]. Rastogi et al. [86] reported that the composites prepared by doping OPL CQDs in NLC (E48) form a more stable suspension as compared to CQDs-7CB composites and investigated the latter for their optical and dielectric properties as well as zeta potential. Authors reported the study of three composites, namely MIX 1, MIX 2, and MIX 3, having OPL CQDs concentrations of 0.1 wt%, 0.2 wt%, and 0.3 wt%, respectively. The positive zeta potential found for MIX 2 and MIX 3 represent the adsorption of H+ ions from the surrounding medium and therefore surface acquired positive charge. However, a negative zeta potential was observed for MIX 1 which was attributed to the release of OH¯ ions from the surrounding medium which imparted negative charge on the surface. Interestingly, the measurements of zeta potential in this study indicated that MIX 1, MIX 2, and MIX 3 are stable suspensions because their zeta potential values are found to be greater than ± 15 mV [87].

It was also observed that the ac conductivity of both pure and composites enhanced with increasing temperature due to a reduction in grain boundary effects and crystal lattice defects as carrier mobility increases. The authors also claimed that, since interfacial space charge polarisation was particularly influential at low frequencies at 0 V, the dielectric properties were substantially affected. Results showed that the dispersed system had more dielectric strength than pure NLC (E48), and exceptionally larger values in the case of MIX 3. The authors believed that the Maier-Meier theory could be helpful in explaining the observed results. The presence of a single curve in Cole-Cole plots for dispersed systems gave proof of a significant grain boundary effect also. Based on these findings, the authors proposed that OPL CQDs-LC composites could be utilized as an improved electrical capacitor with a longer life cycle, low power consumption, quicker charging and discharging rates, cost-effectiveness, etc.

Green synthesis of CQDs has been increasingly popular in recent years since these synthesis methods are cost-effective, easy, and eco-friendly. Eskalen et. al [88] synthesized the fluorescent water-soluble CQDs (∼1.2–3.5 nm) using a hydrothermal process from mandarin juice, a green source, and dispersed them in NLC (E7). The authors investigated three samples, namely S1, S2, and S3, in which 1 ml, 2 ml, and 4 ml of CQDs (10 µg/ml) were dispersed in 300 mg NLC (E7), respectively. The thermal properties of pure NLC (E7) and composites were measured by differential scanning calorimetry (DSC), which was helpful in investigating the behaviour of the phase transition of pure and dispersed NLC composites. DSC results showed that the TN-I (nematic to isotropic phase transition temperature) for pure NLC (E7) samples decreased with the addition of CQDs. The author claimed that the reason for this reduction could be explained using mean-field theory, which predicts that when NLC is dispersed with anisotropic nanoparticles, TN-I follows a variation trend with NPs concentration that can be given by the formula..

Where, T0 is the phase transition temperature of pure LC, ν represents the volume fraction of anisotropic nanoparticles in LC composite, ʋP represents the average nanoparticle volume, W is the coupling constant corresponding to NP-LC anisotropic interaction and w is the coupling constant corresponding to LC-LC interaction. Order parameter was also observed to decrease with doping concentration supporting the DSC results.

In another report, Rastogi et al. [90] dispersed OPL CQDs, which are unique biowaste-based porous CQDs (size ∼ 5.67 nm and pore size ∼ 1.5 mm) in NLC (E48). These novel CQDs (∼6 nm) were synthesized in a single-step pyrolysis technique and then dispersed in NLC (E48) with different wt/wt% concentrations of 0.1, 0.2, and 0.3 %, and named MIX 1, MIX 2, and MIX 3, respectively. The UV–Visible absorption spectra of these dispersed systems revealed that doping of OPL CQDs in E48 caused a red shift in surface plasmon polaritons (SPP) peak when the polarization plane of incident light was parallel to the director of NLC (bright state) and a blue shift corresponding to the dark state. These findings confirmed transmission change in dispersed systems. In contrast, while applying DC bias, a blue shift in the UV–Visible absorption spectra was seen as the switching orientation shifted from the OFF state (0 V) to the ON state (10 V) in the case of the pure NLC (E48). The observed absorption peaks in the UV absorption spectra for pure NLC (E48) and dispersed systems were at 260 nm, 261 nm, and 262 nm, respectively, and a shoulder peak at 320 nm was also noted (Fig. 13 ). The observed values of the bandgap in this study for pure NLC (E48) and dispersed systems were 4.04 eV, 3.89 eV, 3.80 eV, and 3.91 eV, respectively. Reduction in absorbance at high dopant concentration was also noticed. As there were high ionic impurities or excess charge on OPL CQDs which resulted in heating effect and increased conductivity. UV–visible studies demonstrated the direct allowed transition in composite systems. Also, composites had a lower band gap and better UV absorbance than pure NLC (E48), with a little red shift in wavelength. These results make OPL CQD-LC composites promising materials in the field of luminous displays and biosensor devices.

In another report on carbon nanoparticles synthesized from bio-waste and then their dispersion in LC, Pathak et al. [91] synthesized porous carbon nanoparticles (PCNPs) from teakwood materials waste, with the help of a catalyst-free pyrolysis approach and then doped them in a new NLC (VRA–131). The authors prepared three distinct PCNPs-NLC composites with different concentrations of PCNPs in NLC: Mix 1 had 0.1 wt% PCNPs in NLC, Mix 2 had 0.25 wt% PCNPs in NLC, and Mix 3 had 0.5 wt% PCNPs in NLC. The POM analysis of pure NLC and PCNPs-NLC composites demonstrated better homogeneous alignment in composites as compared to pure NLC. This indicated homogenous dispersion of PCNPs into the NLC host, and aggregations at these concentrations were not observed. The authors highlighted the temperature dependence of output intensity and birefringence for pure NLC and its composites (Fig. 14 (a) and 14(b)). The graph clearly demonstrates that birefringence dropped with increasing temperature and approached zero at the isotropic point, meaning that molecular alignment in nematic deteriorated with increasing temperature. The authors also stated that when PCNPs-NLC composites are compared to pure NLC, the value of Δn increases (Fig. 14(c)). Authors proposed that as a result of the porous structure of the PCNPs' surface, NLC molecules were significantly absorbed on their surface, resulting in strong intermolecular forces in PCNPs-NLC interactions and hence improved orientational order which further resulted in the increase of value of Δn. The observed values of Δn were 0.11, 0.13, 0.15, and 0.16 for pure NLC, Mix 1, Mix 2, and Mix 3, respectively. Contrast ratios of composites also increased with increase in the concentrations of PCNPs-NLC. The observed values of the contrast ratio were found to be 44, 52, 58, and 65 for pure NLC, Mix 1, Mix 2, and Mix 3, respectively. An increase in contrast ratio was also observed as a result of in-plane switching for PCNPs dispersed systems. Dielectric studies of pure NLC and composites demonstrated that relative permittivity was increased in composites due to the dominance of dipolar interactions and increased charge storage capacity (Fig. 15 (a) and 15(b)). The observed values of Δε for pure NLC, Mix 1, Mix 2, and Mix 3 were 4.9, 6.5, 7.7, and 8, respectively, indicating that Δε increased with dopant concentration. Overall, these results showed that with increased concentration of PCNPs in LC, order parameter increased resulting in increase in birefringence and contrast ratio, and decrease in threshold voltage. Based on these observations, the authors proposed that these are good alternatives for the production of displays devices, phase shifters, and other electro-optical devices.

It’s worth mentioning here that in addition to NLC and FLC systems, CDs also have the tendency to enhance the charge transport behaviour of discotic LC. Mahesh et al. [92] studied the structural morphology and charge transportation behaviour of CDs dispersed in discotic LC (HAT4) composite systems. The doping of CDs in the HAT4 matrix resulted in a significant increase in the electrical conductivity and decrease in charge carrier mobility. The enhanced electrical conductivity was attributed to the conductive nature of CDs as numerous intrinsic defects are present on their surface, which improved their electronic transportation properties.

ce:section.ce:section-title: CDs doped ferroelectric liquid crystal composites
ce:section.ce:para: In the field of LC research, ferroelectric liquid crystals (FLCs) maintain a superior position due to their quick response time, bistability (i.e., memory), and potential to be transformed into high-contrast display material. Taking into consideration the outstanding optical properties of CDs, Shukla et al. [93] examined the influence of CDs (∼10 nm) doping on the dielectric and electro-optical properties of FLC, which is a phenyl pyrimidine-based LC material. The authors used the crossed polarized optical microscope to examine the samples prepared by dispersing hydrophobic CDs (0.05 and 0.10 wt%) into the FLC and observed that minute amounts of CDs were consistently distributed throughout the FLC matrix and also the phase transition temperatures remained almost unaffected with low concentration of CDs. However, variations in tilt angle of FLCs with CDs were noticed. Moreover, the dispersion of CDs into the FLC host resulted in the improvement of response time (τ), spontaneous polarization (Ps), dielectric permittivity (εꞌ), and an unusual spike in rotational viscosity of FLC.

The increased switching response time was attributed to enhanced rotational viscosity and conductivity of the host FLC. Authors gave two possible explanations for the significant improvement observed in the spontaneous polarization (Ps) and dielectric parameters. The first explanation is that the FLC dipoles aligned themselves parallel to the CDs, causing parallel coupling in them that boosted the net polarization of composites. Moreover, it was observed that composites exhibit higher ac conductivity (σac) as well as higher dc conductivity than pure FLC. Results also showed that there was no noticeable change in σac at the low frequency range but the large conductivity of CDs caused σac to increase at higher frequencies. Authors also reported that with increase in rotational viscosity, theGoldstone mode (GM) relaxation got suppressed.

In another study by, Gangwar et al. [94], the effect of dispersion of CQDs in FLC (KCFLC 10S) on dielectric properties was studied. They prepared three CQDs–FLC composites via two different ways. The first method was dispersion of CQDs (∼4–5 nm) into the FLC host and the other method was deposition of CQDs on firmly anchored substrate surface. Dielectric spectroscopy analysis of these three samples S1 (Strongly rubbed Nylon 6/6 coated substrate), S2 (FLC dispersed with 5 wt% CQDs), and S3 (CQDs deposited on vigorously rubbed Nylon 6/6 alignment layer) were done in order to study their low frequency relaxation process and Goldstone mode. Results showed that as the applied voltage increased, dielectric permittivity (εꞌ) also increased in the pure FLC sample (S1) with maximum value at 300 mV (Fig. 16 (a)) indicating the presence of multiple relaxation processes, which were also confirmed in tan δ vs frequency plot (Fig. 16(b)). The authors believed that the higher-frequency relaxation was the well-known GM, whereas another lower-frequency relaxation was connected to the partially unbound helical mode (p-UHM) and these low-frequency relaxations were responsible for the increased value of εꞌ. As the probing voltage increased, the relaxation frequency of p-UHM increased, indicating that partial unwinding of the helicoidal structure happened at the interface between the sample cell's substrate surface and the FLC. To study the effect of dispersion of CQDs on dielectric properties of FLC, samples S2 and S3 were characterized by high-resolution dielectric spectroscopy. It was observed that the dielectric permittivity was almost independent of the applied probing ac voltages at room temperature. But, small increment in the dielectric permittivity at low frequency was seen. When the dielectric properties of S2 and S3 were compared with S1 at low frequency εꞌ is drastically reduced to about half of the S1 (Fig. 16(c) and 16(e)), and p-UHM was also not observed in the tan δ versus frequency plot (Fig. 16(d) and 16(f)). These results demonstrated that the presence of CQDs suppresses the p-UHM at the interface of FLC and solid substrate surface of the sample cell in both cases, S2 and S3, but only a single dielectric relaxation process (GM) is present in them, which is unaffected by magnitude of the probing ac voltage.

In addition to these studies, the authors also studied the effect of temperature on GM dielectric relaxation process. Their findings revealed that the shift in dielectric relaxation peak towards high frequency was greater in the case of S3 sample due to the surface effect of CQDs in comparison to S2 when CQDs were dispersed into the FLC matrix. This was because the CQDs were more evenly distributed throughout the bulk of the S3 sample. It was hypothesized that the relaxation frequency shift was caused by surface-bound FLC molecules because these molecules were strongly anchored at substrate surface of S3 sample cell as compared to bulk molecules. The authors claimed that the study of contact angle and optical textures at high-resolution results were in agreement with the dielectric study, i.e., CQDs act as suppressors of p-UHM in S2 and S3 configurations.

The contact angle investigation of these samples, S3 revealed a reduction in contact angle (71.8°) in S2 compared to S1 (89°), indicating an increase in surface tension in S2 due to FLC intermolecular interaction mediated by CQDs. For the S1, S2, and S3, they also presented a model for explaining the lack of p-UHM in the case of S2 and S3 configurations (Fig. 17 ). These results have definitely contributed towards a better understanding of interactions between CQDs and FLCs.
Summary of CDs dispersed in various liquid crystal hosts and their modified propertie has been tabulated in Table 3 .

The field of Carbon dots (CDs) has been expanding ever since their discovery and there has been continual development of new methods to harness their outstanding physio-chemical features, optical properties, thermal stability, biocompatibility, good solubility, excellent photo-stability, strong and tunable fluorescence, cost-effectiveness and environmental friendliness. In recent years, due to their unique properties, CDs have been considered as good substitutes to quantum dots and other heavy metal based nanomaterials as dopant materials in LC matrix. And the results obtained are promising. In this review article, we have studied and compiled the recent research work done in the area of CD dispersion in nematic liquid crystals and ferroelectric liquid crystals. Our overall study shows that the use of CDs as dopant in LC matrix resulted in the enhancement of the electro-optical, optical, and dielectric properties of LCs. For instance, CDs (∼8–10 nm) doped in NLC (5PCH) resulted in improved non-linearity properties of the composite systems, making them potential candidates for applications as optical limiters and optical sensor protectors. In the case of CDs-LC1 (Felix-2900–03) composites, reduction in threshold voltage and switching time, reduced dielectric anisotropy, and a high pre-tilt angle values were observed. Moreover, electro-optical properties, DC conductivity and spontaneous polarization of composites were also enhanced. CQDs dispersed in NLC (7CB) systems aided in understanding the fundamental mechanism of CQDs-NLC interaction. CQDs were also reported to enhance the dielectric properties of NLC (E 48). Porous CQDs dispersed in NLC (E48) have the ability to improve memory effect, switching behavior, and electro-optical behavior by improving orientational order of NLC. The oil palm leaf-based CQDs-NLC composites investigation demonstrated the creation of stable suspension in NLC. According to the aforementioned studies, CDs are a better option as eco-friendly dopant than other hazardous conventional QDs, as they have the ability to enhance the liquid crystal's optical, electro-optical and dielectric properties and are helpful in promoting sustainable, heavy metal-free, and environmentally safe technologies.

Despite extensive research on CDs-LC composites, some questions and areas of interest are yet to be addressed. For CDs-LC composites, it is the role of interaction between CDs and LC molecules which eventually results in their enhanced properties. It is of the utmost importance to develop a theoretical model to describe the precise process that governs the interaction of CD molecules and LC molecules in order to provide a more adequate explanation of the observed characteristics. The source of exceptional PL properties of CDs is yet debatable and how they tune PL of LC is again an area that requires detailed study. Moreover, it is also observed that the same CDs derived from different sources influence the properties of LC differently. So, it would be interesting to study the effect of CDs derived from different sources, doping in CDs capping, and the significance ofdimensions and shape of CDs intuning the characteristics of a wide range of LCs to achieve optimum enhancement of their attributes.

In addition to various electrical and optical investigations of LCs and their applications for various electro-optical device applications, topological defects (TDs) in LCs are also studied actively [95,96]. TDs are a prominent feature of LCs with broken rotational symmetry are basically an orientationally disordered point in the assembly of LC molecules wherein the molecular orientation could not be defined. For last few years, TDs in LC materials have been exploited for self-assembly applications. It would be of great interest to study the accumulation of emissive CDs within the cores of topological defects as TDs serve as a means of trapping nanoparticles. Some rudimentary work has been done by Murray et al. where they have utilized functionalized CdSe quantum dots and fluorescent carbon dots as nano emitter aggregation in topological defects [97]. But more detail studies are required to study CDs accumulation in TDs and investigation of the dynamics of defects and nanoparticles in liquid crystals and for their potential applicability in analyzing the decomposition of defects into multiple defects etc.

Thus, there are still so many opportunities to pursue further research on CDs-LC composites. In addition, we are of the opinion that the future looks promising for CDs-LC composites as luminous and energy harvesting materials, in particular for the manufacture of affordable and ecologically friendly electro-optical equipment.
